question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
Objective: To analyse current evidence on the efficacy of bandage systems containing both elastic and inelastic components (mixed-component systems).


Inclusion Criteria: Primary quantitative, qualitative and mixed-methods studies and case studies series exploring the efficacy of mixed-component compression systems; English language publications; published between 2005-2015; and ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) range between 0.8-1.3.

question: Were eligibility criteria unambiguous?
answers: Inclusion Criteria: Primary quantitative, qualitative and mixed-methods studies and case studies series exploring the efficacy of mixed-component compression systems; English language publications; published between 2005-2015; and ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) range between 0.8-1.3.

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers: Exclusion Criteria: Secondary studies exploring the efficacy of mixed-component compression; studies exploring mixed-component compression in the treatment of nonvenous or mixed-aetiology ulcers (i.e. ABPI I <0.8 or above 1.3); studies published out with date limits; non-English language publications; and studies exploring MCSs in the treatment of lymphoedema.

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers: Exclusion Criteria: Secondary studies exploring the efficacy of mixed-component compression; studies exploring mixed-component compression in the treatment of nonvenous or mixed-aetiology ulcers (i.e. ABPI I <0.8 or above 1.3); studies published out with date limits; non-English language publications; and studies exploring MCSs in the treatment of lymphoedema.

answers:

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers: Subsequent electronic and hand searches were also carried out at library resource centres; however, no further studies were identified for inclusion.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers: The following keywords and terms were applied both singularly and in various combinations with truncations to allow all possible noun and verb endings: venous leg ulcer*, varicose ulcer*, leg ulcer*, compression, bandag*, elastic, inelastic, short stretch, healing rate*, interface pressure, mixed component*, two-layer, four-layer and multi-layer

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers:
limits were set between 2005-March 2015 to eliminate literature that would not be relevant.


English language publications

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Although the review revealed a sufficient amount of evi-dence relating to the efficacy of MCSs, three points shouldbe noted regarding potential limitations relating to the stud-ies included. First, all eight studies failed to address and ade-quately control clinical skill in bandage application as aconfounding variable that could affect the perceived efficacyof a bandage system and therefore final results

Second, all but one paper were sponsored by industryproducing either MCSA or MCSB which may have led topotential bias towards promotion of their efficacy. Andthird, none of the studies explored the combination of elas-tic and elastic components as an innovative 'best of both'technology and bandage category which may distinguish itfrom alternative systems using purely elastic or inelasticcomponents in terms of therapeutic efficacy
Strengths and limitations of the review
As there is so little existing literature exploring the collec-tive evidence for MCSs as a compression bandage systemwithin its own distinct category, this review has synthesisedcurrent evidence to reveal an increased understanding oftheir efficacy for consideration of further research. Had theauthor been able to access a research team to support largerstudy, a more comprehensive picture of the efficacy ofMCSs might have been obtained. Following completion ofthe review, new evidence comparing MCSA to MCSB alsoemerged; a retrospective analysis of patients' records in theUK found both systems to be associated with positive out-comes including cost-effectiveness, ulcer healing and QoL(Guestet al.2015). The design of the study would havepotentially contributed towards answering the researchquestion as it would have strengthened the variation inmethodologies included in the review, whilst additionaldata on cost-effectiveness of MCSs would have been usefulas it was not an outcome in any of the studies included inthis review.

question: Was the relevance of identified studies to the review's research question appropriately considered?
answers:
The aim of this systematic literature review was to explore the currently available evidence for MCSs containing both elastic and inelastic materials (MCSA and MCSB; Table 2) commonly used in the treatment of venous leg ulcers


Inclusion Criteria: Primary quantitative, qualitative and mixed-methods studies and case studies series exploring the efficacy of mixed-component compression systems; Englishlanguage publications; published between 2005-2015; and ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) range between 0.8-1.3.


Exclusion Criteria: Secondary studies exploring the efficacy of mixed-component compression; studies exploring mixed-component compression in the treatment of nonvenous or mixed-aetiology ulcers (i.e. ABPI I <0.8 or above 1.3); studies published out with date limits; non-English language publications; and studies exploring MCSs in the treatment of lymphoedema.

question: Did the reviewers avoid emphasizing results on the basis of their statistical significance?
answers: Yes

question: Did the research questions and inclusion criteria for the review include the components of PICO?
answers: Inclusion Criteria: Primary quantitative, qualitative and mixed-methods studies and case studies series exploring the efficacy of mixed-component compression systems; English language publications; published between 2005-2015; and ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) range between 0.8-1.3.

question: Did the review authors explain their selection of the study designs for inclusion in the review?
answers: Although RCTs and quantitative literature should provide valuable sources of collated numerical, statistical data necessary to assist in determining the outcomes of the research question, qualitative studies, most notably those pertaining to evidence surrounding patient concordance, comfort and quality of life, may be the more appropriate paradigm from which to explore themes, descriptive data and the relationships between the researcher and participants in determining outcomes for compression (Topping 2006). As many studies relating to compression therapy seek evidence on outcomes relating to both quantitative and qualitative data, studies using mixed-methods design might also be of relevance (Pluye & Hong 2013). Much of the literature supporting wound care is in the shape of consensus documents and case studies, both of which focus upon the strength of expert opinions and experiential knowledge in the gathering of relevant evidence. A seminal work by Carper (1978) cited four fundamentals of nursing knowledge including empirics, ethics, aesthetics and personal knowledge which were proposed to carry equal weight in clinical decision-making. Therefore, the hierarchy of evidence applied for the purposes of this review will be as follows: 1 RCTs. 2 Cohort/Case controlled studies. 3 Qualitative or mixed-methods studies. 4 Case studies in series. Case studies were only considered for inclusion if they were presented in a series format to maximise the strength of quantitative data. The rationale for this was that single case studies would likely be too numerous and lacking in appropriate wider applicability.
